q: Fluid is a substance that offers no permanent resistance to deforming forces.
Fluid statics concentrates on fluids in motion.
Hydrodynamics is the study of liquid at rest.
The volume of a gas depends on pressure and temperature.
The density of a liquid is constant for any pressure provided the temperature is kept constant.
The path or flow of fluid particles is called a streamline.
In laminar flow, streamlines cross each other frequently.
In steady laminar flow, every fluid particle arriving at a given point has the same velocity.
Turbulent flow is characterized by smooth paths in layers.
In turbulent flow, the speed of the fluid at a point is continuously undergoing changes in both magnitude and direction.
Internal friction in fluids is called viscosity.
Ideal laminar fluid flow assumes the fluid is compressible.
Ideal laminar fluid flow ignores or neglects friction.
In an ideal fluid, the density of the fluid is constant.
The mass of liquid entering one end of a pipe must equal the mass flowing out the other end.
The volume flow rate is the same throughout a pipe for an ideal fluid.
The equation $A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$ is known as the equation of continuity for fluid flow.
The larger the area of the pipe, the faster is the speed of the fluid.
Bernoulli's equation relates pressure, flow speed, and height for the steady flow of an ideal fluid.
Bernoulli's equation was first derived by a Swiss mathematician.
Bernoulli's equation can be derived using the work-energy principle.
In Bernoulli's equation, the work done on a section of fluid equals the change in total mechanical energy.
The sum of pressure energy, kinetic energy, and potential energy per unit volume remains constant in streamlined laminar flow.
Air moving above the top curved surface of an aeroplane wing travels a shorter distance than air below.
The velocity of air flow is low along the top surface of a wing and high along the bottom surface.
A lift on an aeroplane wing is provided by high pressure below the wing and low pressure above.
In an insect sprayer, fast moving air at the nozzle tip causes high pressure.
The Magnus effect is associated with a spinning object moving through a fluid.
The Magnus effect was named after a German physicist who described it in 1852.
During cyclones, high-speed air above a roof causes increased pressure on the roof's exterior.
Most fluids are ideal and offer no resistance to motion.
Viscosity measures resistance to flow arising from internal friction between fluid layers.
A fluid with high viscosity flows easily because its molecular make up results in very little friction.
Gases do not exhibit viscosity under ordinary circumstances.
The viscosity of liquids decreases rapidly with an increase in temperature.
The viscosity of gases decreases with an increase in temperature.
Viscosity is an intensive property that does not change with the amount of matter.
The SI unit of viscosity is the pascal second ($Pa \cdot s$).
Dynamic viscosity is measured by a fluid's resistive flow under the weight of gravity.
Newton's law of viscosity states shear stress is directly proportional to the negative velocity gradient.
Shear stress is defined as the shearing force per unit area.
Stokes' law describes the drag force on large cubic objects in a fluid.
Terminal velocity is reached when upward and downward forces on a falling object are in balance.
Surface tension causes the surface of a liquid to behave like a stretched elastic skin.
Cohesion refers to forces between molecules of different substances.
The SI unit of surface tension is newton per metre ($N \cdot m^{-1}$).
Capillary action is the spontaneous flow of a liquid into a narrow tube.
Adhesion refers to forces between molecules of the same substance.
Capillarity occurs for all liquids, including non-wetting ones.
Mercury is a liquid that wets glass capillary tubes.
If the adhesive force is greater than the cohesive force, the water level in a tube rises.
The rise of water underneath the ground is an example of capillary action.
